title: Legacy
text:
The village of Kazematsu was a quiet sanctuary, nestled within a mountain valley that sheltered it from the beasts roaming the wilds beyond. Pine-scented air drifted through the fields, carried by the soft rush of the river winding past terraced farmland. Beneath the boughs of an ancient tree, the sharp clack of stones against a wooden board mingled with the distant shouts of training disciples, sounds woven into the rhythm of daily life.


This village was special, renowned across the region for producing geniuses. That reputation was no accident. it was the first village ever founded and established by the Ten Great Sages themselves. While other settlements passed down martial techniques, weapon crafts, or bloodlines, Kazematsu had inherited something older: the teachings, principles, and spiritual discipline left behind by those legendary figures.


Before children here learned to read, Elders guided them through simple stances and breathing exercises to nurture the early growth of their souls. A Soul Tome formed slowly during these years, a spiritual book recording memories, talents, and the faint beginnings of strength. Yet every time the binding thickened or the level grew, a quiet toll was taken, anchoring a shard of their youth directly into the parchment.


Even this innocent process was shadowed by imperial law. The Empire forbade any village from letting a cultivator rise above Level 25, an unspoken limit revealing the dread the rulers held for fully awakened Souls.


At the apex of cultivation stood the Sages, legendary beings surpassing thousands of levels, their minds and bodies sharpened beyond mortal comprehension. To reach that threshold required decades of discipline, insight, and supernatural mastery over one’s own essence. Such figures were treasures to the land, yet their presence also invited imperial scrutiny, which usually ended in destruction.


Each year, the Empire sent envoys to these valleys under the guise of recruitment. The villagers knew better. Hushed tales spoke of exceptional girls taken as Soul Furnaces, their vitality drained to empower stronger tyrants, and boys pressed into service as front-line fodder. Whether true or not, the shadow of those rumors weighed heavily on every household.


Yet the village lived on, fragile but steady. The seasons circled the peaks with a patient rhythm. But for some families, the Empire’s reach lingered more sharply, etched into the memories within their very souls.


Kai, fifteen, with messy black hair falling across his forehead, knew this intimately. Beside him rested his father’s Tome, its cover cracked, the faint glow of its binding long dimmed. He traced a finger along the spine, inhaling the scent of ancient parchment. At Level 5, he was considered a prodigy among the youth, yet the words within his father’s legacy swirled in half-formed symbols, tantalizing and dangerous.


Only by fully absorbing a Tome could one access its knowledge. To attempt it prematurely invited total annihilation. His father had hunted the high peaks, a warrior far beyond Kai’s current depth. Forcing open that book now would shatter Kai’s mind. His bones would twist, his essence ripping into fragments until he became a mindless beast—a hollow hunger roaming the wastes. For now, the Tome remained closed, waiting for the day he could safely claim it.


Across from him, Lina leaned forward, her golden hair catching the sunlight filtering through the pine needles. Her brown eyes flashed with mischief, scanning the Go board with a precision that mirrored her sharp mind. A year younger and Level 4, she was rivaled only by Kai. At such tender ages, their souls burned with extraordinary potential, the exact brilliance that drew the Empire’s hunger. Kai felt a cold dread every single year, knowing the rumors surrounding girls with her potential. To him, the threat was never distant speculation.


Lina placed a black stone with a decisive clack, breaking the silence. “Your turn,” she said, flipping her hair back to hide a grin. The trap was laid. “Don’t take too long. I plan to win before sunset.”


Kai studied the board. He knew her tells, learned through countless matches. She wanted him to act quickly and make a misstep. But he moved with care, sealing her black group and fortifying his own territory. He built formations like walls of mountains, while Lina sought to capture every individual piece.


A cool breeze stirred, rustling the canopy above. The grooves along the ancient pine pulsed faintly, almost imperceptibly. Kai paused, his hand hovering. “Did you feel that?”


“The tree… it reacted,” Lina whispered, eyes wide.


Legends claimed the pine had once served as a cultivation focus for a Sage long before the current Elders arrived. It hummed softly, a heartbeat buried in the bark, a silent witness to centuries of vigilance.


A calm voice, weighted with authority, drifted across the clearing. “You two seem remarkably sensitive today.”


They leapt to their feet, bowing instantly. “Master!”


Their instructor approached, his robes flowing, his age betraying no weakness. To the villagers, he was a guardian and the caretaker of orphans. To Kai, he was far more: a figure whose skill had survived the outside world long enough to grow ancient.


The old man looked down at the grid, pointing a finger at their stones. “The Principle of Reflection never lies. Your moves mirror your inner worlds perfectly. Lina, your chaotic, aggressive stones show a spirit defined by survival. And Kai, your defensive walls show a desperate need to anchor and protect.” He looked up, his expression hardening. “But a karmic balance can only withstand so much tension before everything breaks.”


“Why did the tree pulse?” Lina asked, unable to hide her curiosity.


“Even ancient things stir when the world beyond grows restless,” Master replied. His eyes, dark and hard to read, swept the forest line.


“Beasts?” Kai ventured.


Master’s lips curved faintly, though the warmth did not reach his eyes. “Let’s pack up.”


Lina’s frown deepened. “Master? The game isn’t finished. I was winning.” pouting.


“Leave the board,” he commanded, the tone sharp enough to make her flinch.

Kai secured his father’s Tome in his sash, his heart tightening. He had never seen their Master so grave.


“What is it?” Kai asked, stepping protectively toward Lina. “A beast? Did one cross the mountains?”


Master turned back, his gaze hollow. “Worse. Beasts feed on flesh. What comes now feeds on potential.”


A tense quiet fell, heavy as the peaks themselves. The sun lowered, gilding the valley in orange and gold. Flowers scented the air and villagers finished their work, yet an undercurrent of unease flowed through the soil.


Walking back, Kai and Lina stayed side by side. Master followed, a silent mountain of vigilance behind them. Villagers bowed as they passed, some whispering, some offering small smiles. For the two orphans, this man was a father, a protector, and a guide who had shaped their hands, trained their bodies, and nurtured their souls.


Kai had lived all his years in Kazematsu, first with his father, then with Master after his father’s disappearance. Lina had come from elsewhere, her home, family, and past stolen by the regime. She had wandered, lost and broken, until the old man had taken her in. Time had forged her into strength, but the shadow of her loss lingered in every glance.


“Do you think they’d bow to me if they knew how often I beat you?” Lina whispered, trying to break the tension.


Kai rolled his eyes, smiling despite himself. “Probably just to thank you for humbling me.”


Their conversation died near the village square, where a frantic crowd had gathered. Elder Huan approached Master, his voice trembling.


“The sentries on the peaks… they signaled,” Huan said, pale.


“How many?”


“Thousands. Flying the Empire’s banner.”


Kai felt a chill race down his spine. Lina’s voice faltered. “The Empire? What do they want now? Another child? Another family torn apart?”


Master’s gaze was heavy. “The Empire grows like a weed, consuming all. It fears what we represent. Wisdom. Independence. The refusal to bow blindly.”


He spoke then of the Sages of old, men like Oren the Rooted Scholar, Vaelis the Seeker of Truth, and Kaelen the Last Oracle, figures who wielded both strategy and blade to protect others. Hearing those names, the villagers’ fear melted into pride, determination coiling in their hearts.


Lina drifted to the edge of the crowd, eyes fixed on the horizon. Kai followed quietly.


“Something’s coming,” she whispered. “Master knows. He’s preparing us, but he cannot stop it. And whatever it is, it will change everything.”


A cry shattered the moment. A boy ran into the square, his breath ragged.


“Smoke! In the distance!”


Dark plumes rose above the treetops, staining the sunset. Lina’s arms hugged her torso as memories of her past surged unbidden. “I don’t like this,” she said, trembling. “The last time I saw that much smoke, I lost everything.”


Master’s eyes lingered on the horizon. “Go home. Stay inside. Hold your families close.”


Then, turning to Kai and Lina, his voice carried both sorrow and urgency. “You two. Come with me. There is something you must know.”


They followed silently, the temple looming ahead, shadows stretching long across the courtyard. With every step, the air grew heavy, pressing down like an omen. Neither could shake the sense that tonight, the game had changed, and nothing would ever be the same again.
